comments.text: I put together a write-up that settles the first two questions of Erdős Problem #456 unconditionally, and the third question conditionally on Dickson's conjecture for one explicit admissible triple of linear forms. The writeup is at this Overleaf link Specifically: (Q1, Q2) Unconditional: there is a constant $c > 0$ such that $\#\{n \le x : m_n = p_n\} \ge cx$ for all sufficiently large $x$. In particular, the assertions "$m_n < p_n$ for almost all $n$" and "$p_n/m_n \to \infty$ for almost all $n$" both fail, on a set of positive lower density. No prime-tuple conjecture is used in this part of the proof. (Q3) Dickson-conditional: assume Dickson's conjecture for the admissible triple of linear forms $t$, $2t+1$, $8t+1$. Then there are infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p-1$ is the unique integer $n$ with $m_n = p$. More precisely, whenever $\ell$, $2\ell+1$, and $8\ell+1$ are all prime, the prime $p = 8\ell+1$ has this uniqueness property. Thus, I claim a (mixed) solution to this problem: the first two questions are settled outright, and the third is settled conditionally on a standard prime-tuple hypothesis stemming from Dickson's conjecture. The manuscript is lengthy (71 pages); all in all, the Q3 Dickson-conditional construction occupies only about two pages (Section 3), while the remaining sixty-odd pages (Sections 4 through 11 and the three appendices) are devoted to the unconditional Q1/Q2 proof. Some brief remarks on what is novel. The unconditional Q1/Q2 result rests on a weighted-logarithmic-density tightness theorem for an auxiliary divisor sum that controls the cofactors arising in the construction. This theorem is built by transferring Fan and Pomerance's natural-density tightness for the shifted-prime-divisor function $\omega^*(n)$ to weighted logarithmic density with fixed multipliers, by handling a squarefree large-ratio tail through a fractional Euler-product moment, and by treating the early-prime contribution as a level-set bound on a multiscale normal set (which is what allows the argument to proceed without any unrestricted first-moment claim). Downstream the architecture is 'standard': a dyadic arithmetic-progression form of Goldfeld's large-prime-factor argument, with uniformity for $b \le (\log X)^A$ and built from Siegel-Walfisz, Bombieri-Vinogradov, and Brun-Titchmarsh, supplies the base population; two- and three-linear-form upper-bound sieves which are applied only in their long-interval regimes, with line-by-line divisor-weight averages over the relevant coefficient range in the latter case, delete the bad witnesses; and a second-moment Cauchy-Schwarz step deduplicates the survivors into distinct integers. By contrast, the Dickson-conditional Q3 result, is elementary: the maximal-divisor characterization of $p$ being a uniqueness prime reduces the question, for $p = 8\ell + 1$, to checking that $\varphi(3(2\ell+1)) = 4\ell$ and $\varphi(15) = 8$ both fall below $8\ell$, which they do whenever $\ell$, $2\ell+1$, $8\ell+1$ are all prime; the triple $(t, 2t+1, 8t+1)$ is admissible, so Dickson supplies infinitely many such $\ell$. The proof was developed via an automated multi-turn audit-and-revise scaffold that I built, which iteratively queried ChatGPT-5.5-Pro across several hundred rounds, including a substantial post-review pass to put everything together. I did not carry out the turn-by-turn reasoning by hand; the scaffold itself is my engineering contribution, and the final proof has been checked by myself these past couple of days. Here is ChatGPT-5.5-Pro verifying the solution: check 1, check 2, check 3. As you can see, GPT-5.5 Pro cannot find any errors, but it hedges to admit correctness due to the length of the solution. When pressed to scrutinize the solution even more, it does so and concludes the solution is correct, including in its novel parts. Still, I appreciate if human expert reviewers could weigh in. Very appreciated would also be if anyone with access to very good autoformalization harnesses could help, since I imagine the formalization of this proof would be at least many tens of thousands of lines in Lean.

Unless I'm missing something, we always have $\phi(p_n) = p_n - 1 \equiv 0 \pmod{n}$, so that $m_n \le p_n$ for all $n$. So I'm guessing that Erdős meant to say that $p_n > m_n$ holds infinitely often. In this case, take $n = 2^{2k+1}$. Since $\phi(2n) = n$, we have $m_n \le 2n$. On the other hand, $n+1$ is divisible by $3$, so that $p_n \ge 2n+1 > m_n$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Yes apologies, this was a typo on my part, I used the wrong inequality sign.
